name: First Steps CLI App
description: Build a tiny command-line Python app that prints your name and a welcome message.
instructions: Create a Python file called main.py. Store your name in a variable, print your name, and print a second welcome line. Run it from the terminal.
goals: Create and run a Python file
Use variables to store simple data
steps: Create a file named main.py
Store your name in a variable
Print the variable
Print a second welcome line
Run the file in your terminal
deliverables: A Python file that runs without errors
A welcome message printed below it
starterTips: Keep it simple — the goal is confidence and setup
Use print(name) rather than typing the name twice
Edit the welcome text and rerun the script to test changes

stages.label: Print your name and welcome message

name: Mini Mad Libs Generator
description: Collect words from the user and generate a funny sentence.
instructions: Ask the user for a name, verb, adjective, and place. Then print a silly story using those values.
steps: Choose 4 or 5 words to collect from the user
Store each response in a variable
Build one final sentence using all responses
Print the result cleanly
deliverables: A script that asks for several words
A final generated sentence or short story
starterTips: Keep the story short and funny
You can join text with commas in print() or use +

stages.label: Read the brief and choose your word prompts

name: Build a Calculator
description: Create a simple Python calculator that can add, subtract, multiply, and divide.
instructions: Build a calculator program in Python. Ask the user for two numbers and an operation, then show the result. Handle invalid operations cleanly.
goals: Use conditionals to choose the right operation
Return or print the result clearly
steps: Ask the user for the first number
Ask the user for the second number
Ask for an operator like +, -, * or /
Use if / elif / else to decide what to do
Print the result in a readable format
deliverables: Support for at least four operations
starterTips: Start without functions, then refactor into functions later
Remember input() returns text, so convert numbers using int() or float()
Test division carefully

stages.label: Read the brief and plan supported operations
Collect both numbers and the operator
Run at least 3 successful test cases

name: Number Guessing Game
description: Build a guessing game where the player tries to guess a hidden number.
instructions: Generate or choose a target number, collect guesses from the user, and keep looping until the player gets it right.
goals: Use a loop for repeated guesses
Compare each guess against a target
Give feedback like too high or too low
steps: Choose a target number
Ask the user for a guess
Use a while loop until the guess is correct
Print hints after each wrong guess
Celebrate when the guess is correct
deliverables: A success message at the end
starterTips: Start with a fixed target number, then randomize it later
Think carefully about when your loop should stop

stages.label: End the game correctly on success

name: Study Score Tracker
description: Store scores in a list and calculate the average, best score, and lowest score.
instructions: Create a list of study scores and write logic that prints useful stats about the list.
goals: Work with a list of numbers
steps: Create a list of at least 5 scores
Calculate the total
Compute the average
Find the highest and lowest score
Print a clean report
starterTips: You can do it manually first, then refactor with loops
Keep variable names very clear

name: To-Do List CLI
description: Create a small command-line to-do list that can show and add tasks.
instructions: Use a list to store tasks. Let the user choose options like view tasks and add a task.
steps: Create an empty list of tasks
Show a small text menu
Let the user add a task
Let the user view tasks
Allow the user to exit the app
deliverables: A task list stored in Python
starterTips: Start with view/add/quit only
Add task numbering for cleaner output

stages.label: Keep the app running until quit

name: FizzBuzz Challenge
description: Solve the classic FizzBuzz challenge with clean conditional logic.
instructions: Print numbers from 1 to 30. For multiples of 3 print Fizz, for multiples of 5 print Buzz, and for both print FizzBuzz.
steps: Loop through numbers 1 to 30
Check divisibility by 3 and 5
Handle the combined case first
Print the correct output for each number
starterTips: Check the both-case before the individual cases
Use % for divisibility

name: File Rename Planner
description: Plan a small script that renames files using a consistent pattern.
instructions: You do not need real files at first. Simulate a list of filenames and write code that outputs their renamed versions using a naming rule.
goals: Work through a practical scripting problem
steps: Create a list of fake filenames
Choose a consistent naming pattern
Loop through the list
Generate and print the renamed versions
Keep output readable
deliverables: A matching list or output of renamed files
starterTips: Start with fake data before touching real files
This is about thinking like an automator, not filesystem complexity yet

stages.label: Print original and new names clearly

name: Simple Quiz Engine
description: Create a mini quiz game with at least three questions and a score counter.
instructions: Store a few questions and answers, ask the player each one, and track how many they get right.
steps: Choose at least 3 questions
Store answers in a clean structure
Ask each question one at a time
Check user input against correct answers
Print a final score summary
deliverables: A quiz with at least 3 questions
starterTips: You can begin with parallel lists, then upgrade to dictionaries
Think about lowercase comparison for easier matching

name: Budget Breakdown Tool
description: Create a small program that totals spending across categories and reports the biggest one.
instructions: Use a dictionary where keys are categories and values are amounts. Print the total spend and identify the largest category.
goals: Extract useful insights from simple data
steps: Create a spending dictionary
Calculate the total of all categories
Find the category with the largest value
Print a concise spending report
starterTips: Keep categories simple like food, travel, books
Build the total first, then solve the largest-category part

name: Portfolio Generator
description: Build a Python-driven project summary tool that prints a mini portfolio in the terminal.
instructions: Create a Python script that stores project names, skills, and short descriptions, then prints them in a clean portfolio-style format in the terminal.
goals: Format output so it feels deliberate and readable
Build something that resembles a real showcase tool
steps: Create a list of project dictionaries
Give each project a name, description, and list of skills
Write a function to print one project neatly
Loop through all projects and print them
Add a polished heading and section separators
deliverables: A script that prints at least three projects
Each project should include a title and short description
Each project should show related skills
starterTips: A list of dictionaries is a great structure for this
Try writing one display function and reusing it
Think of this as preparation for building real portfolio tools later
